programs.name: Oregon Health Plan (OHP)

eligibility.income_limits: For elderly (65+), eligibility is primarily through OSIPM (Oregon Supplemental Income Program-Medical), part of OHP for long-term care. Income limits for Aged, Blind, and Disabled (ABD) category in 2025: $967/month for an individual, $1,450/month for a couple (based on Federal Benefit Rate). General OHP Plus for adults 19-64 is up to 133% FPL (~$1,507/month single, $3,076 family of 4; updated annually). OHP Bridge for adults 19-65: 139-200% FPL (e.g., single: up to ~$2,674/month). Limits vary by household size and program; full table example from CareOregon (approximate monthly, subject to annual FPL updates): Family of 1 - OHP Plus $1,800, Bridge $2,674; Family of 2 - higher accordingly. Elderly long-term care has specific thresholds; apply to confirm.[3][4][5]
eligibility.asset_limits: OSIPM for elderly long-term care has asset limits (not detailed in exact dollar amounts here, but typically Medicaid-standard ~$2,000 individual/$3,000 couple excluding home, one car, personal items, burial plots). Ways to qualify if over limits via spend-down or exemptions. What counts: cash, bank accounts, investments; exempt: primary home (if intent to return), one vehicle, household goods.[3]
other_requirements: Oregon resident
U.S. citizen or eligible immigration status (as of July 2023, some benefits regardless of status)
For long-term care: need for nursing home (30+ days), assisted living, adult foster care, or home-based services; meet disability/blind criteria if applicable
Not eligible if access to affordable insurance (for Bridge)
Verification of income/resources required post-application[1][3][6]

benefits.value: Comprehensive no-cost coverage: medical, dental, prescription drugs, behavioral health. For elderly/long-term care via OSIPM: nursing home care, assisted living, adult foster care, home health/non-medical support services to stay at home. Nursing home residents keep ~$77/month personal needs allowance ($90 with VA pension). Covers frail seniors' needs.[3][4][6]

methods: Online: ONE.Oregon.gov or Benefits.Oregon.gov
Phone: 1-800-699-9075 (ONE Customer Service); for older adults/disabilities: 1-855-ORE-ADRC (855-673-2372)
Mail: OHP Customer Service, P.O. Box (details on site)
In-person: Local Oregon Department of Human Services (ODHS) office or Aging and Disability Resource Connection (ADRC)
application.processing_time: Not specified in sources; apply anytime, eligibility determined after submission.
forms: Online application via ONE.Oregon.gov; paper application mailed to OHP Customer Service
documents: Proof of income (pay stubs, tax returns), identity (ID/driver's license), residency, citizenship/immigration status, assets/resources, disability/medical records if applicable, Medicare info if dual-eligible[3][6]

geography.regional_variations: Administered statewide by ODHS, but local offices handle in-person; potential regional differences in providers, wait times for long-term care services (not detailed)[3][6]
offices_or_providers: Local ODHS offices; ADRC centers; CareOregon as coordinated care organization in some regions

gotchas: Multiple OHP tiers (Plus, Bridge, OSIPM for elderly LTC) with different limits—elderly often need OSIPM, not standard Plus; ABD has stricter FBR-based limits vs. FPL; asset tests apply for LTC, not always for general OHP; income calculated current/next/annual depending on program; Medicare dual-eligibility affects drug coverage (OHP Limited Drug); spend-down options if over limits; FPL updates annually—2026 guide referenced but limits shown through 2025; apply even if over limits as exemptions/strategies exist[1][3][4][9]
programs.data_shape_notes: benefits scale by household size and tier (OHP Plus/Bridge/OSIPM); income uses FPL or FBR; long-term care for elderly via OSIPM with asset tests and spend-down; statewide but local ODHS/ADRC handling

programs.name: APD Medicaid Long Term Services and Supports

eligibility.income_limits: For single applicants in 2026: $2,982 per month. Limits vary by marital status and program (e.g., slightly different for K Plan and CEP under ABD Medicaid). Couples or households have adjusted limits based on Oregon Health Plan rules, but specific tables not detailed; consult OHP for full household breakdowns.[1][2]
eligibility.asset_limits: For single applicants in 2026: $2,000. Counts most assets; exemptions typically include primary home (if equity under certain limits), one vehicle, personal belongings, and burial funds (standard Medicaid exemptions apply). Spousal protections allow higher community spouse asset allowance.[1][2]
other_requirements: Nursing Facility Level of Care (NFLOC) determined via Client Assessment and Planning System (CAPS) assessing ADLs/IADLs.
For APD Waiver: Service Priority Level (SPL) 1-13; primarily for those 65+ or 18-64 physically disabled transitioning from nursing home to community.
Aged, blind, or disabled for basic ABD Medicaid; functional assessment for long-term services.[1][2][3]

benefits.value: Home and community-based services including Waiver Case Management, Community Transition Services (one-time setup for institutional transitions), Housing Support Services, personal care, homemaker services, home modifications, adult foster care, assisted living, and non-medical supports to avoid institutionalization. Specifics via K Plan (state plan option) or 1915(c) APD Waiver; no fixed dollar amounts or hours—personalized based on assessment.[3][6]

methods: Phone: Contact local Aging and Disability Resource Connection (ADRC) or APD office (numbers via oregon.gov/odhs).
Online: Oregon Health Plan application at benefits.oregon.gov (ONE system).
Paper/Mail: Form 7210 Application for Oregon Health Plan (OHP) Benefits.
In-person: Local APD offices or ADRCs.[7][8]
application.processing_time: Not specified; functional assessment via interview (in-person or phone).[4]
application.waitlist: Yes, due to limited funding; varies by eligibility category, service availability, and area. Highest need prioritized via SPL.[1][4][9]
forms: Form 7210: Application for Oregon Health Plan (OHP) Benefits (replaces 539A for paper apps). CAPS assessment form for NFLOC.[8]
documents: Proof of income (pay stubs, SSI/SSDI awards).
Proof of assets (bank statements, property deeds).
ID (birth certificate, Social Security card).
Medical records or doctor statements for disability/functional needs.[4]

geography.restrictions: None statewide, but access depends on local funds, programs, and caregivers.
geography.regional_variations: Wait times and provider availability vary by area; some regions have longer waitlists.[1][4]
offices_or_providers: Local APD offices and Aging & Disability Resource Connections (ADRCs) statewide via oregon.gov/odhs.

gotchas: Not an entitlement—waitlists common unlike basic ABD Medicaid coverage.
Must be transitioning from nursing home for core APD Waiver; SPL 1-13 required.
K Plan and CEP have slightly different financial limits than standard ABD.
Functional NFLOC assessment via CAPS is mandatory; prepare for ADL/IADL interview.
Income over limit? Spousal impoverishment rules or spend-down may apply, but consult planner.[1][2][3][4]
programs.data_shape_notes: Waiver-based with priority tiers (SPL 1-13); waitlisted and area-dependent; NFLOC via CAPS assessment; separate from entitlement ABD Medicaid.

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits. Fully covered for Medicaid-eligible individuals. Non-Medicaid participants with Medicare pay a monthly premium (amount not specified in sources). Private pay option available for those ineligible for Medicare or Medicaid.
other_requirements: Reside in the PACE program's approved service area
Assessed at Nursing Facility Level of Care (Oregon Service Priority Level 1-13)
Able to live safely in the community (own home, community-based care, or CBC facility) with PACE services without jeopardizing health/safety of self or others
Medicaid eligible or willing to pay private fees
Willing to receive all health and long-term care exclusively from PACE providers
Not eligible if requiring enhanced care or long-term care at Oregon State Hospital, or Medicare skilled care with no community discharge expected within 30 days

benefits.value: All Medicare and Medicaid services plus long-term care: primary medical care, prescription drugs, adult day services, occupational/physical therapy, social work, in-home services, community-based care (CBC) facility care, nursing home care (if enrolled), palliative care, medical equipment/supplies, dietary services. Delivered via interdisciplinary team at health/social centers or home/community settings. No deductibles or copays for PACE-provided services.

methods: Contact PACE organization intake specialists for screening and enrollment (specific providers handle intake)
No central statewide phone or form specified; contact local PACE provider (e.g., Providence ElderPlace for Multnomah/Clackamas/Washington/Clatsop areas)
documents: Documentation for nursing facility level of care assessment (Service Priority Level 1-13), proof of residency in service area, Medicaid/Medicare status if applicable

geography.restrictions: Limited to PACE-approved service areas (e.g., Multnomah, Clackamas, Washington, Clatsop counties via Providence ElderPlace; other providers may serve additional areas)
geography.regional_variations: Services delivered by specific PACE organizations (e.g., Providence); availability, centers, and intake vary by provider/service area. Not available statewide.
offices_or_providers: Providence ElderPlace (Multnomah, Clackamas, Washington, Clatsop counties)
Other PACE organizations in Oregon (exact list not in sources; contact ODHS or local AAA)

gotchas: Must use ONLY PACE providers for all care (no outside doctors/services)
Limited to specific service areas; must live there or relocate
Nursing home level assessment required (SPL 1-13 in Oregon); not for those needing state hospital care
Non-Medicaid pay premium (cost varies/not specified)
Enrollment can occur in home/CBC/nursing facility, but must be safe in community with PACE
programs.data_shape_notes: county-restricted to PACE service areas with multiple providers; no financial eligibility test; NFLOC via Oregon SPL 1-13; private pay option

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (MSP) via OHP

eligibility.income_limits: MSP has three tiers with different income thresholds[6]: (1) QMB (Qualified Medicare Beneficiary): $1,330/month for individual, $1,804/month for couple (100% of federal poverty level); (2) SLMB/SMB (Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary): $1,596/month for individual, $2,164/month for couple (120% of poverty level); (3) QI/SMF (Qualifying Individual): $1,796/month for individual, $2,435/month for couple (120-135% of poverty level). Income limits increase annually in March[6]. Note: ODHS starts with gross income and subtracts $20[8].
eligibility.asset_limits: As of January 1, 2016, there are no resource limits for QMB programs in Oregon[3][8]. However, for Oregon Health Plan (OHP) eligibility more broadly, Medicare members must have $2,000 or less in countable assets[2][10].
other_requirements: Must be enrolled in Medicare Part A (hospital insurance)[1][6]
Income and resources must be within eligibility limits[3]
If eligible for any other Medicaid benefit offered by the State of Oregon, you are not eligible for the SMF benefit[6]
Individuals in nursing facilities, intermediate care facilities for the mentally retarded (ICF/MR), or hospitals are not eligible for QMB-SMB if income is 120% or greater of FPL[1]

benefits.value: QMB pays for Medicare Part A and B premiums, deductibles, and co-insurance[6]. SLMB/SMB pays for Part B premiums[6]. QI/SMF pays for Part B premiums[6].

methods: Contact your nearest Aging and Disability Office[3]
Online application available through Oregon Health Plan system
Phone application available
In-person application at local offices
application.waitlist: SMF benefit has enrollment caps: 'We are only allowed to serve a certain number of people each year for the SMF benefit. Unfortunately, we must shut down enrollment if we reach our limit.'[6]
documents: Birthdate (birth certificate if available)[7]
Social Security number (if applicable)[7]
Income and deduction information (pay stubs)[7]
Immigration documentation (if applicable)[7]
Information about health insurance available through employer (if any)[7]

offices_or_providers: Aging and Disability Offices (specific locations not detailed in search results)[3]

gotchas: SMF (QI) benefit has annual enrollment caps and may close if limits are reached[6]
If someone qualifies for ANY other Medicaid benefit from Oregon, they cannot use SMF[6]
Income limits are recalculated annually in March[6]
ODHS uses gross income minus $20 as the starting point for calculations[8]
People often don't realize they may qualify even if they think their income is too high[8]
No resource limits for QMB programs in Oregon as of 2016, but this differs from other states[3][8]
Medicare is primary insurance; MSP is supplemental[3]
programs.data_shape_notes: MSP is a three-tier program with escalating income thresholds and different benefit levels. The program is statewide but has enrollment caps on the SMF tier. Income limits are tied to federal poverty levels and adjust annually. Unlike some states, Oregon eliminated resource limits for QMB programs. The program requires Medicare Part A enrollment and excludes those eligible for other Medicaid benefits (for SMF tier). Processing timelines and specific regional office locations are not detailed in available sources.

## Critical Information for Families

**What This Program Actually Is:** Medicare Savings Programs (MSP) are **not** a separate health plan—they're **cost-sharing assistance** that helps Medicare beneficiaries pay their premiums, deductibles, and co-insurance[6]. Think of it as a supplement to Medicare, not a replacement.

**The Three Tiers Explained:** The program has three levels based on income[6]:
- **QMB** (most help): Covers Part A/B premiums, deductibles, and co-insurance
- **SLMB/SMB** (moderate help): Covers Part B premiums only
- **QI/SMF** (limited help): Covers Part B premiums only, but has enrollment caps

**Critical Gotcha #1 — SMF Enrollment Caps:** The SMF tier (highest income tier) has a **hard enrollment limit each year**. Once Oregon reaches its cap, they stop accepting applications[6]. If someone misses the window, they may have to wait until the next year.

**Critical Gotcha #2 — The Medicaid Exclusion:** If your elderly loved one qualifies for **any other Medicaid benefit** (including OHP Plus), they **cannot** use the SMF benefit[6]. This is a common trap—people assume they can stack benefits, but they can't for SMF.

**Critical Gotcha #3 — No Resource Limits (But Confusing):** Oregon eliminated resource/asset limits for QMB programs in 2016[3][8], which is unusual. However, broader OHP eligibility still has a $2,000 asset limit[2]. The distinction matters depending on which tier they qualify for.

**How to Start:** Contact your **nearest Aging and Disability Office**[3]. The state form is **DHS 9017**[8]. Income limits are recalculated every March, so timing matters[6].

programs.name: SNAP Food Benefits

eligibility.age: No minimum age requirement for household members, but special rules apply for households with members 60 or older

income_limits.description: Gross income limit is 200% of federal poverty level (Oct. 1, 2025 through Sept. 30, 2026)[1]
income_limits.special_rule_for_elderly_or_disabled: If household has member 60+ or with disability and exceeds gross income limit, can qualify instead by meeting Net Income and Asset tests[1]
income_limits.net_income_calculation: SNAP benefits are based on net income; approximately $100 more in net income = $30 less in benefits[1]

asset_limits.general_rule: House, car, and money in bank usually do not count against eligibility[2]
asset_limits.specific_exemptions: Search results do not provide detailed asset limit thresholds, but indicate assets are generally not a barrier to qualification

other_requirements: Must live in Oregon[2]
Must be U.S. citizen or eligible non-citizen[2]
Eligible non-citizens must meet specific criteria: lived in U.S. for 5 years, under age 18, worked 40 qualifying work quarters, blind/disabled, or 65+ and legally present since Aug. 22, 1996[2]
Able-bodied adults without dependents (ABAWDs, ages 18–64) must work, volunteer, or train for at least 80 hours/month to remain eligible for SNAP[3]

benefits.value: Variable based on household net income; benefits are provided as food purchasing power on EBT card (or check/direct deposit in some cases)[1][2]
benefits.additional_details: Minimum and maximum SNAP benefit amounts exist but are not specified in search results[1]. Households with member 60+ can deduct out-of-pocket medical costs to increase benefits[2][6]. Older adults (65+) can have full shelter costs included when determining benefits[2]

methods: Online: Through Oregon Department of Human Services (ODHS) website[9]
Phone: Contact ODHS (specific phone number not provided in search results)[9]
In-person: At local ODHS office[9]
Mail: Application by mail option available (specific address not provided in search results)[9]
application.processing_time: Search results do not specify standard processing time. Expedited benefits available: if approved, can receive benefits within 7 days if applicant qualifies for expedited SNAP[4]
application.expedited_eligibility: Qualify for expedited SNAP if: (1) monthly income + cash total less than rent/mortgage + utilities, (2) monthly income under $150 with less than $100 in cash/bank, or (3) farmworker with less than $100 in cash[4]
application.waitlist: No waitlist mentioned in search results
application.forms: Specific form names/numbers not provided in search results; application checklist available on Oregon.gov[9]
documents: Proof of out-of-pocket medical costs (if claiming medical deductions for applicant 60+ or disabled)[2][6]
Documentation of work, volunteer, or training hours (for ABAWDs)[3]
Proof of citizenship or eligible non-citizen status[2]
Search results reference 'checklist' on Oregon.gov but do not detail all required documents

geography.restrictions: Work requirements (ABAWD) rolled out in six counties October 1, 2025 (Multnomah, Washington, Clackamas, Benton, Hood River, Yamhill) with statewide rollout starting January 2026[3]

regional_variations.direct_deposit_check_ebt_card_option: Adults 65+ in Clackamas, Columbia, Multnomah, and Washington counties can receive SNAP benefits as direct deposit, check, or EBT card[2][6]
regional_variations.cash_benefits_option: If everyone in household is 65+ or living with SSI/SSDI disability, benefits issued as cash in Multnomah County for flexibility[7]

geography.offices_or_providers: Oregon Department of Human Services (ODHS) administers program statewide; local ODHS offices handle applications and benefits

gotchas: Only about half of eligible seniors apply for SNAP despite qualifying; low participation rate among older adults[4]
Able-bodied adults without dependents (ages 18–64) must now work/volunteer/train 80 hours/month or lose benefits after 3 months; this requirement is rolling out by region starting October 2025[3]

SNAP eligibility changes from H.R. 1 removed eligibility for certain refugee, asylee, and lawfully present immigrant groups; check ODHS page for current list[3]
Existing SNAP participants were notified of benefit changes starting October 15, 2025[3]
For older adults with mobility issues, a trusted person can purchase food on their behalf using their SNAP benefits via alternate payee form[2][6]
SNAP can be used at community meal sites like Meals on Wheels, not just traditional grocery stores[7]

## Key Takeaways for Families

**For elderly loved ones specifically:** Oregon's SNAP program is particularly favorable for seniors 60+. If your elderly relative exceeds the gross income limit, they can still qualify by meeting alternative Net Income and Asset tests instead[1]. Additionally, out-of-pocket medical costs can be deducted from income, potentially qualifying them for more benefits—this is a significant advantage many seniors miss[2][6].

**Income limits are generous:** At 200% of federal poverty level, Oregon's limits are higher than the federal baseline[1]. For a single person, the gross income limit is $2,608/month[1].

**Assets rarely disqualify applicants:** Your house, car, and bank savings typically don't count against eligibility, making this program accessible to many seniors with modest savings[2].

**Application is flexible:** You can apply online, by phone, in-person, or by mail through ODHS[9]. If your elderly relative needs help, a trusted person can use their SNAP benefits to purchase food on their behalf with an alternate payee form[2][6].

**Processing can be fast:** While standard processing time isn't specified, expedited benefits are available within 7 days if your relative qualifies (income under $150 with less than $100 in cash, or income + cash less than rent/utilities)[4].

**Regional benefit delivery varies:** In Multnomah, Washington, Clackamas, and Columbia counties, seniors 65+ can receive benefits as direct deposit, check, or EBT card—not just EBT[2][6]. In Multnomah County specifically, if all household members are 65+ or have SSI/SSDI disability, benefits are issued as cash[7].

programs.name: Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP) / Oregon Energy Assistance Program (OEAP)

eligibility.income_limits: Household gross income at or below 60% of Oregon State Median Income (SMI), based on household size. Effective October 1, 2025 (most recent available): 1: $38,385 annual/$3,198.75 monthly; 2: $50,196/$4,183; 3: $62,006/$5,167.17; 4: $73,817/$6,151.42; 5: $85,627/$7,135.58; 6: $97,438/$8,119.83; 7: $99,652/$8,304.33; 8: $101,867/$8,488.92; 9: $104,081/$8,673.42; 10: $106,296/$8,858; 11: $108,510/$9,042.50; 12: $110,725/$9,227.08; each additional: +$2,215 annual/+184.58 monthly. For single-person households, greater of 60% SMI or full-time Portland minimum wage. Gross income before deductions. Levels based on prior 30 days income[2][4][5].
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits mentioned in program guidelines[2][5].
other_requirements: Oregon resident
Documented energy costs (e.g., heating/cooling bills from any fuel source including wood, oil; must have utility bill)
Homeowners and renters eligible (rental/utility agreements and landlord cooperation may affect benefits)
Priority for seniors 60+ and/or documented disabled, especially homebound[2][3][5]

benefits.value: One-time grant applied directly to energy provider for heating/cooling bills; may include repair/replacement of unsafe heating systems; energy education, case management, home weatherization services. Amount not fixed statewide—varies by need, funding, local design[2][5].

methods: Phone: Toll-free 1-800-453-5511 (option 2); email: energyassistance@oregon.gov[1][2]
Contact local Community Action Agency by county (find via 800-453-5511 opt 2 or oregon.gov/ohcs)[1][2][5]
In-person/phone/mail/home visits (especially for homebound); appointments prioritized for 60+/disabled[2][3][5]
application.processing_time: Not specified; funds limited, apply early[2][5][7]
application.waitlist: Possible due to limited funds; priority for elderly/disabled may reduce wait[3]
forms: Declaration of Household Income (Zero Income Statement) for members 18+ with no/irregular income[3]
documents: ID for all adults 18+
Social Security cards/numbers for all household members
Proof of income for prior calendar month (e.g., Social Security benefit letter; call 1-800-772-1213 if needed)
Most recent energy/utility bill in applicant's name (all providers, even if not primary)

geography.restrictions: None; available statewide via local agencies
geography.regional_variations: Administered by local Community Action Agencies; policies, procedures, benefit design vary slightly by county to meet local needs; different providers/offices per region[2][5]
offices_or_providers: Local Community Action Agencies across Oregon (contact via 800-453-5511 opt 2 to locate by county)[1][2][5]

gotchas: Priority given to seniors 60+ and disabled—others may face longer waits or limited funds[3]
Must have documented energy costs; rental/landlord issues can limit benefits[5]
Income based on recent 30 days—recent income drop may qualify you[7]
No fixed benefit amount; varies by local agency, funding availability[2][5]
Homebound can request accommodations, but must contact local agency[2]
Separate from LIHWA (water assistance); coordinate if eligible[1]

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program (WAP)

eligibility.income_limits: Household gross income at or below 200% of the federal poverty level. Income includes all household members before payroll deductions. Specific dollar amounts vary annually and by household size; contact local agency for current table as 2025-26 guidelines not detailed in sources. Priority given to seniors (60+), people with disabilities, households with children under 6, high energy users, and high energy burden households.
other_requirements: Renters need landlord approval.
Homeowners or renters in single-family homes, mobile homes, or multifamily (with approval).
Must meet LIHEAP eligibility or similar for certification in some cases.

benefits.value: Free weatherization services including: ceiling/wall/floor insulation, air sealing/infiltration reduction, furnace/heating system repair or replacement, heating duct improvements, energy-related minor home repairs, base-load measures, health/safety repairs, ventilation, LED bulbs, and energy conservation education. Services determined by energy audit for cost-effectiveness.

methods: Contact local Community Action Agency (CAA) or subgrantee by phone or in-person.

Examples: Clackamas County (phone not specified in results), Marion/Polk (503-361-8740 press 0), Multnomah (503-282-6309), Washington (503-640-3497), Yamhill (503-472-0457). Portland homeowners: Community Energy Project (phone not specified). Lane County: Homes for Good. Mid-Willamette: MWVCAA.
Mail or in-person: Varies by local agency, e.g., NeighborImpact application packet.
No statewide online application; agency-specific forms like NeighborImpact Weatherization Application, Supplemental form, Declaration of Household Income.
application.processing_time: Not specified; agency contacts eligible households to schedule energy audit after application.
application.waitlist: Households may be placed on a waiting service list after contacting local agency.
documents: Proof of income (paycheck stubs for 60 days, Social Security/SSDI/SSI benefit letter).
Utility bills (electric, gas if applicable).
Proof of ID for members 18+ (Driver’s License, Passport, etc.).
Proof of ownership/rental (mortgage statement, deed, lease).

geography.restrictions: Administered locally; available to renters/homeowners statewide.
geography.regional_variations: Administered by local community action agencies, senior centers, housing authorities; different providers, phone numbers, and waitlists by county/region. Examples: NeighborImpact (Deschutes?), Homes for Good (Lane County), MWVCAA (Mid-Willamette), county-specific CAAs.
offices_or_providers: Local Community Action Agencies by county
Oregon Housing and Community Services (OHCS) oversees

gotchas: Priority populations (elderly 60+, disabled, young children) get preference; non-priority may face longer waits.
Renters must get landlord permission.
Services based on energy audit cost-effectiveness, not all measures guaranteed.
Income at exactly 200% FPL qualifies; gross income before deductions.
May certify eligibility via LIHEAP or other programs without re-verifying income.
Waiting lists common due to funding limits.
programs.data_shape_notes: Administered by county-specific subgrantees with varying contacts; priority tiers affect service timing; income fixed at 200% FPL with no asset test; services vary by audit results

programs.name: Senior Health Insurance Benefits Assistance (SHIBA)

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits; open to all Oregon residents with Medicare regardless of income.
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits; no asset test applies.
other_requirements: Must be an Oregon resident with Medicare.
Includes people age 65+ and individuals ages 18-64 with disabilities who have Medicare.

benefits.value: Free, objective, unbiased counseling and education on Medicare benefits, options, billing issues, appeals, plan comparisons, rights, and identifying Medicare fraud, error, or abuse. Provided one-on-one by trained volunteer counselors via phone, in-person, online, or public events. No financial aid, healthcare services, or fixed hours/dollar amounts.

methods: Phone: 800-722-4134 (toll-free statewide hotline; enter zip code for local routing)
Website: https://shiba.oregon.gov/get-help/pages/helpnearyou.aspx (find local counseling sites)
Email: shiba.oregon@odhs.oregon.gov or online contact form at https://shiba.oregon.gov/get-help/pages/connect-with-us.aspx
In-person: Local offices and events (search by zip code on site; examples include Clackamas: 503-655-8269, Multnomah: 503-988-3646)
Public events: Medicare presentations at libraries and offices (check local calendars)
application.processing_time: Immediate counseling available by phone or appointment; no formal application processing.
forms: No specific application forms required for receiving help; volunteer applicants complete SHIBA Volunteer Application.
documents: No documents needed to receive counseling; bring Medicare card, current plan details, bills, or questions for personalized assistance.

geography.restrictions: None; available to all Oregon counties.
geography.regional_variations: Delivered through statewide network of local offices, sponsors, and volunteers with county-specific phone lines, events, and sites (e.g., Clackamas, Multnomah); no differences in services, but local availability and scheduling varies.
offices_or_providers: Statewide office: Department of Consumer and Business Services, 350 Winter Street NE Room 330, Salem OR 97309-0405
Local sites searchable by zip code at https://shiba.oregon.gov/get-help/pages/helpnearyou.aspx

gotchas: Not a benefits-paying program—provides only free counseling, not financial assistance, healthcare, or enrollment on your behalf.
Requires Medicare enrollment; helps with Medicare questions but does not cover non-Medicare insurance.
Services rely on volunteer availability; during peak times like Open Enrollment (Oct 15-Dec 7), schedule appointments early.
People with Medicaid/OHP changes post-COVID emergency may need to transition to Medicare and use SHIBA for guidance.
Confidential but assessors review resources/needs during sessions.
programs.data_shape_notes: no income or asset test; volunteer-based counseling service scaled statewide via local sites with no fixed benefits value

eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits mentioned in sources.

Lack family or other natural supports for meal preparation[1][3]
For Medicaid HDM: Medicaid eligible, receiving long-term services in home, assessed via CA/PS[3][4]
Spouse or disabled dependent child may qualify if it benefits the homebound individual[1][2]
Reside in program delivery zone[2][5]

benefits.value: Home-delivered nutritious meals (typically hot, sometimes frozen; meet daily nutritional standards approved by dietician; one meal per delivery serving recommended daily allowance). Number/frequency not specified statewide (e.g., authorized monthly quantity for Medicaid). May include fees on sliding scale or free for low-income[1][2][3][4]

methods: Contact local Area Agency on Aging (AAA) by phone for assessment[1][2]

In-person initial assessment by case manager[1]

geography.regional_variations: Administered by local AAAs with varying providers, delivery zones, fees, and processes; e.g., Council on Aging (Central Oregon), Meals on Wheels People (Portland area)[1][5]

Council on Aging (Central Oregon)[1]
Meals on Wheels People (Multnomah/Washington/Clark Counties: 503.953.8111 or 866.788.6325)[5]

## Key Context for Families

**What This Program Actually Does:**
SCSEP is a **transitional employment bridge**, not a permanent benefit[3][4]. Participants work part-time (averaging 20 hours per week) at minimum wage in community service roles—such as schools, hospitals, day-care centers, and senior centers—while receiving job training and employment support[4]. The program pays participants to gain work experience while helping them transition to permanent, unsubsidized employment[3].

**Critical Eligibility Barrier:**
The 125% federal poverty level income cap is the primary gatekeeper[3][4]. This threshold is substantially lower than typical senior incomes. For example, in 2025, this would exclude most seniors with even modest Social Security or retirement income. Families must contact providers directly to learn the exact dollar limits for their household size.

**Application Reality:**
There is no single online application portal. Families must either call the toll-free Department of Labor line, contact Easter Seals Oregon directly, or visit the Eugene office in person[1][2][4]. The search results do not specify processing times, waitlist status, or required documentation—information families will need to obtain directly from providers.

**Regional Limitation:**
While SCSEP operates statewide in Oregon through Easter Seals, the search results only document one physical office location (Eugene)[2]. Families outside Eugene should clarify whether services are available in their area and whether they must travel or apply remotely.

**What's Missing:**
The search results do not provide: current income thresholds in dollars, asset limits, processing timelines, required documents, or how regional variations affect service delivery. Families should expect to gather this information through direct contact with providers.

programs.name: Oregon Senior Tuition Waiver (OSU/UO Audit Program)

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits or asset limits apply. No variations by household size.
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits. No assets counted or exempted as none required.
other_requirements: Oregon resident
Must audit classes (no credit or grade earned)
Instructor permission required
Space available after paying students register
Not for limited entry programs (e.g., Nursing, Aquarium Science)
Maximum 8 credits per term at some colleges

benefits.value: 100% tuition waiver for audited credit classes. Does not cover student fees, course fees, lab fees, or materials. Up to 8 credits per term possible.

methods: Varies by institution: e.g., at Oregon Coast CC - online application, email instructor (cc Registrar@OregonCoast.edu), submit Senior Waiver form online; at UO - submit Auditor Registration Form and Staff Tuition Benefit form through Registrar's Office (specific URLs/forms per school)
Contact specific college registrar for details (no central phone listed)
application.processing_time: Registration after first day of class if approved; instructor response time varies
application.waitlist: No formal waitlist; space-available only after paying students
documents: Proof of age (e.g., ID showing 65+)
Proof of Oregon residency
Online application if new student

geography.restrictions: Available at public universities (e.g., OSU, UO, PSU) and community colleges statewide
geography.regional_variations: Process varies by institution (e.g., email instructor at OCCC, forms at UO); some community colleges may have age 60+ (e.g., OCCC), but statute mandates 65+ for universities/community colleges
offices_or_providers: University of Oregon Registrar
Oregon State University (contact registrar)
Community college registrars (e.g., Oregon Coast CC Registrar@OregonCoast.edu)

gotchas: No credit earned - purely audit/no grade
Fees still apply (mandatory, course, lab)
Instructor must approve; seats only if available post-regular registration
Cannot displace paying students or enter limited programs
Some colleges cap at 8 credits/term
Community colleges may allow 60+ locally, but state law 65+ for uniformity
programs.data_shape_notes: Statewide but institution-administered; audit-only (no credit); no financial tests; space/instructor gatekept; age 65+ per statute (some colleges 60+)

programs.name: Health Related Social Needs Benefits (HSRSN)

eligibility.income_limits: For housing benefits (rent/utilities, storage, tenancy): Household income 30% or less of the area median income (AMI) where you live, plus lack resources/support to prevent homelessness. Varies by location; no full table provided in sources. Other services (e.g., nutrition) may have food security assessments but no fixed income limits specified.[1][3][4]
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits mentioned; focuses on lacking resources/support networks to prevent homelessness for housing services.[3][4]
other_requirements: Must be enrolled in Oregon Health Plan (OHP)/Medicaid.[1][2][4][5][6]

In at least one qualifying life transition: release from incarceration past 12 months; discharge from IMD past 12 months; current/past Oregon child welfare involvement; transitioning to dual Medicaid/Medicare (next 3 months or past 9 months); homeless/at risk of homelessness; youth 19-26 with special health care needs (starting 2025).[1][2][3][5][6]

Plus clinical/health risk factors for specific services: complex physical/behavioral health condition, developmental/intellectual disability, difficulty with self-care/ADLs, abuse/neglect, 65+, under 6, pregnant/postpartum, high crisis service use, etc.[1][2][3][4][5]

benefits.value: Housing: rent/utilities up to 6 months, storage fees, medically necessary home mods (e.g., ramps, grab bars), pest control/deep cleaning, hotel costs. Nutrition: medically tailored meals for low/very low food security (requires dietitian/PCP assessment). Weather: supports for extreme weather. Must not duplicate other state/local/federal benefits.[3][4][5][6]

methods: Contact healthcare provider or local OHP community partner.[3]
CCO-specific: PacificSource request form (via 211info); Health Share, AllCare, etc. via their sites.[4][5][8]
Unite Us Assistance Request Form (referral).[6]
OHP Open Card: care coordination.[7]
Check OHP status: 1-800-273-0557 or local ODHS.[6]
Apply for OHP if needed: https://www.oregon.gov/oha/HSD/OHP/Pages/apply.aspx[6]
application.processing_time: Varies by service/provider; high volume causes longer times (e.g., PacificSource processing in order received; Lane County pre-1/24/2026 requests ongoing as of early 2026).[4]
application.waitlist: Not explicitly mentioned; processing delays noted due to volume.[4]
documents: Proof of OHP enrollment.
Lease/written landlord agreement (for housed housing support).[4]
Documentation of life transition, health conditions, income (for housing: showing ≤30% AMI).[1][3][4]

geography.restrictions: Available statewide to eligible OHP members; housing income threshold based on local AMI.[1][3]
geography.regional_variations: Administered via regional Coordinated Care Organizations (CCOs) like IHN, PacificSource (Lane County specifics), Health Share, AllCare, Advanced Health; processing/wait times vary by CCO/provider; OHP Open Card for non-CCO members.[2][4][5][6][7][8]
offices_or_providers: Regional CCOs
Local OHP community partners
211info for some CCOs

gotchas: Not for emergencies (e.g., imminent eviction: contact local Community Action Agency instead).[1]
Strict federal requirements; not all OHP members qualify—must match exact transition + clinical criteria.[1][2][3]
Cannot duplicate other benefits; housing requires being currently housed with lease.[4][6]
Varies significantly by CCO/provider; check your specific CCO.[4][5]
Takes time to approve; high volume delays.[1][4]
programs.data_shape_notes: OHP/Medicaid-exclusive; eligibility ties specific life transitions to clinical risks per service; housing income at 30% local AMI; CCO-administered with regional processing variations; no age minimum but includes elderly (65+) as clinical factor.
